flows.title: Phishing recovery
flows.lead: If you clicked a phishing link or entered information, act fast.

steps.title: Disconnect from networks
steps.description: If you're worried malware was installed, turn off Wi-Fi and mobile data while you finish these steps.

steps.title: Change the password you typed
steps.description: Open the real site (not from the link) and reset the password. If you reused that password elsewhere, change those too.

steps.title: Turn on two-factor authentication
steps.description: Add a code generator app or hardware key. SMS-based 2FA is better than nothing but can be SIM-swapped.

steps.title: Review sign-in activity
steps.description: Most accounts (Google, Apple, Microsoft) show recent device sign-ins. Sign out any device you don't recognize.

steps.title: Check connected apps
steps.description: Revoke any third-party app permissions you don't recognize in account security settings.

steps.title: Run a malware scan
steps.description: Use Play Protect on Android, the built-in scanner on Windows, or your antivirus on Mac.

steps.title: File a report
steps.description: Report to your country's fraud authority — ScamRadar can prefill this for you.

flows.title: Romance scam recovery
flows.lead: Stop sending money or gifts. The person is not who they say they are.

steps.title: Cut contact
steps.description: Block the account on every platform: chat app, dating app, social media, email, phone.

steps.title: Stop any payments in flight
steps.description: Call your bank, card issuer, gift-card brand, Western Union, MoneyGram, Zelle, or crypto exchange to attempt a reversal. Speed matters.

steps.title: Save evidence
steps.description: Screenshot every conversation, payment receipt, and profile before you block them. ScamRadar's Evidence Locker can bundle it.

steps.title: Reverse-image search photos
steps.description: Confirm to yourself the photos were stolen. This protects you from re-engaging if they reach out again.

steps.title: Tell one trusted person
steps.description: Romance-scam shame keeps people silent. Telling one person breaks the isolation that scammers rely on.

steps.title: File a report
steps.description: FTC (US), Action Fraud (UK), or your national fraud agency. Also report to the platform where you met.

flows.title: Tax-agency impersonator recovery
flows.lead: The real IRS, HMRC, ATO, or CRA does not threaten arrest by phone or text.

steps.title: Don't pay
steps.description: If you haven't paid yet, stop. Real tax agencies use postal mail and never demand gift cards or crypto.

steps.title: Reverse payment if possible
steps.description: If you paid by gift card, call the brand's fraud line immediately. By crypto, contact the exchange. By bank transfer, call the bank.

steps.title: Verify your real tax status
steps.description: Sign in to the real tax-agency website using a bookmark or typed URL — never the scammer's link.

steps.title: Report to the tax agency
steps.description: US: phishing@irs.gov + TIGTA. UK: phishing@hmrc.gov.uk. AU: ReportEmailFraud@ato.gov.au. CA: anti-fraud centre.

steps.title: Place a fraud alert
steps.description: Contact one major credit bureau (US: Equifax/Experian/TransUnion) to place a free fraud alert.

steps.title: File with the fraud authority
steps.description: ScamRadar can prefill the right form for your country.

flows.title: Crypto scam recovery
flows.lead: Crypto is fast but not always lost. Move quickly.

steps.title: Stop sending more
steps.description: Pig-butchering scams escalate. The 'small fee to withdraw' is another scam step.

steps.title: Document the transaction IDs
steps.description: Copy every transaction hash and wallet address. These are needed for any chance of trace.

steps.title: Report to the exchange
steps.description: Coinbase, Binance, Kraken, and others have fraud lines. They cannot reverse, but they can flag the receiving wallet.

steps.title: Report to authorities
steps.description: FBI IC3 (US), Action Fraud (UK), or your national cyber-fraud unit.

steps.title: Avoid 'recovery services'
steps.description: Most are secondary scams. Real recovery requires court orders, not a Telegram contact.

steps.title: Save evidence
steps.description: Screenshots, wallet addresses, chat logs. ScamRadar's Evidence Locker bundles these.

flows.title: "Family emergency" / grandparent scam recovery
flows.lead: The voice you heard may have been AI-cloned. Verify the real family member is safe.

steps.title: Call your relative directly
steps.description: Use a number you already have, not one from the scammer.

steps.title: Stop the payment
steps.description: Bank wire, Zelle, gift card, or cash courier — call the bank/brand immediately. Couriers can sometimes be intercepted by police.

steps.title: Tell other family members
steps.description: Scammers often try multiple relatives. Warn the rest of the family today.

steps.title: Set a family code phrase
steps.description: Agree on a word only your family knows. Real future emergencies will use it. ScamRadar's verified-contact feature is built for this.

steps.title: Report to authorities
steps.description: FTC (US), Action Fraud (UK), CAFC (CA), or local police if money was lost.

flows.title: Fake delivery scam recovery
flows.lead: USPS, FedEx, UPS, Royal Mail, AusPost never ask for fees via SMS link.

steps.title: Don't pay the 'redelivery fee'
steps.description: Even small fees are pretexts to capture your card and identity details.

steps.title: Change card if you entered details
steps.description: Call the issuer to freeze or replace the card.

steps.title: Verify the real tracking
steps.description: Use the carrier's app or website directly. Don't tap any link from the SMS.

steps.title: Report the SMS
steps.description: Forward to 7726 (US/UK/CA carriers). Then delete.

steps.title: Place a credit lock
steps.description: If you provided personal info, a free credit freeze blocks new accounts.

flows.title: Fake job-offer recovery
flows.lead: Real employers never charge fees, never pay via gift card, and verify identity in person.

steps.title: Stop talking to them
steps.description: Block the recruiter on every platform you contacted them on.

steps.title: Reverse any payment
steps.description: If you paid for 'equipment' or 'training', call your bank or card issuer for chargeback.

steps.title: Cancel any checks deposited
steps.description: Fake-check scams clear initially then bounce. The bank will charge it back.

steps.title: Protect your identity
steps.description: If you sent ID documents, place a fraud alert with credit bureaus.

steps.title: Report to platforms
steps.description: LinkedIn, Indeed, Glassdoor — report the fake recruiter and company.

steps.title: File a fraud report
steps.description: FTC ReportFraud, IC3, or local equivalent.

flows.title: Tech-support scam recovery
flows.lead: Real Apple, Microsoft, Google never call you. Browser pop-ups with phone numbers are always fake.

steps.title: Disconnect if remote access was granted
steps.description: Unplug your network cable or turn off Wi-Fi to break their session.

steps.title: Uninstall remote-access tools
steps.description: AnyDesk, TeamViewer, LogMeIn — anything installed during the call. Then reboot.

steps.title: Change passwords from a clean device
steps.description: Bank, email, password manager — change from another device they don't control.

steps.title: Reverse payment
steps.description: Call the bank or card issuer immediately. If you paid in gift cards, call the brand's fraud line.

steps.title: Run malware scan
steps.description: Windows Defender full scan, or Mac Malwarebytes.

steps.title: Report
steps.description: FTC (US), Action Fraud (UK), CAFC (CA), or local cyber-fraud unit.

flows.title: Lottery / prize scam recovery
flows.lead: Real lotteries never charge you to receive a prize you didn't enter.

steps.title: Stop paying
steps.description: Any 'tax', 'shipping', or 'processing fee' to claim winnings is the scam.

steps.title: Reverse payments
steps.description: Bank wire, gift card, crypto — call the relevant fraud line. Speed matters.

steps.title: Block the scammer
steps.description: Phone, email, social media — every channel.

steps.title: Protect your identity
steps.description: Place a fraud alert with credit bureaus if you provided ID.

steps.title: Report
steps.description: FTC (US), or your national fraud agency.

flows.title: Investment scam recovery
flows.lead: If they guarantee returns, it's a scam. Real investments have risk disclosures.

steps.title: Stop adding funds
steps.description: Don't 'invest more to recover'. That's the second-stage scam.

steps.title: Attempt withdrawal anyway
steps.description: Document the response — refusal or fees are clear evidence.

steps.title: Save records
steps.description: Every email, screenshot, transaction. ScamRadar's Evidence Locker bundles these.

steps.title: Report to securities regulator
steps.description: US: SEC.gov tips. UK: FCA. AU: ASIC. CA: CSA. They also coordinate with criminal authorities.

steps.title: Report to your bank
steps.description: If you wired or transferred to the scammer, file a fraud claim with your bank — required by some recovery routes.

steps.title: Beware recovery scams
steps.description: Anyone messaging you offering to 'recover' funds is almost always a follow-on scam.
